No. 12

THE DREAMER.
Marsa’s Lisrary, St. PATRICK’S, DUBLIN,

When clouds from Shadowland descend
And memories whisper in his ear,
He seeks some bock instead of friend.
To find a far more wholecsome cheer.
The covers open in his hand,
And open gates of IMairyland!

His books are friends—his friends are books, :
All old and faded, fat and thin;
He little heeds their outiward looks,
With the sweet thoughts that lie within.
For though their coats are frayed and old,
They wrap round many a heart of gold.

Hearts that once thrilled to hopes and fears
The joy of life; the blot, the blur:

In dreams he treads the twilight years,
Axnd feels the dust of ages stir:

Then ghosts step in from far and ‘wide

Of those who lived and loved and died.

Ho dreams again, and lo! Romance
Starts quivering from her mouldering urm,
And Chivalny’s steel-flaming lance
And stately, nodding plumes return.
Then long dead poets wake with song,
This pilgrim from the starry throng.
RANDAL MACDONNELL.

PRINTING IN MONAGHAN, 1801-25,
In the introduction to my YCTast of .Iiéo-kas, ctc., printed in Monaghap

in the Eighteenth Century ”’ (2nd ed., Dundalk, 1906), 1 traced the
rise and progress of printing in that town down to 1800. I now con-

' tinue the. list for the next quarter of a century.

tioned in the “‘List,”’ we find Stephen Goggin at work down to 1803, and

Joseph Robinson, who had been In partnenship with Duffy, busy
throughout the whole period, with Nathaniel Greacan, whose imprint
first appears in 1807 as a competitor. Many of the items are the little
song books, for the production cf which the town was so long noted.
Few of these have survived, as from their flimsy paper and mrcula..tlon
from hand to hand, they soon wore out, and specimens, as a rule, are

IRISH LITERARY SOCIETY.
‘CoMING OF AG¥, CLURDRATIONS. , !
The twenty-first anniversary of the foundation of the Society Wa8

eelebmted with much enthumamn and sucCess in the lectureo theatre 1
~ of University College, London, on 10th June. In the afternocon Mr.

~ W. B. Yeats—supported by Lady Gregory and Mr. A. P. Graveﬁ—-—Pm",;f
ﬁldﬁd over & 1‘“‘39 gathering, When Mr. T, W. R:o]ilnston: Vico-l"l‘eﬁlde“th

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 903

dveﬂli'i'er:;d an address on ‘‘ Irish Thought and Art for Twenty-one
Years.” 1 he speaker, who was warmly received and loudly applauded,
gave a delightful résumé of the great progress made in Ireland in Art,
til'_w Drama, Music, Literature, the Arts and Orafts, since the incepé
tion of this Society and its compeer in Dublin, the National Literary
Societyy. Mr. Yeats recalled how, in the dark winter of 1891-92, Ire-
land, its high hopes dashed and ite outlook darkemed, was rent and
torn with dissension. That, he felt, was an appropriate time to attempt
to turn men’s thoughts from the fierce field of politics to the higher
realms of literature, where ail could again unite on a common basis.
He was told! it was the wrong time, but he held it was the right, and
in spite of many rebuffs succeeded, as the gathering there that day
proved.

In the evening Mr. A. P. Graves, the President, delivered a bril-
liant lecture on: ‘‘ Ireland’s Share in: the Folk Somg Revival,” tracing
| the influences of lrish songs in their three great varieties, from the
earliest times, through Dante and Shakespeare, Beethoven and Moz-
art, to the prescot day. He was ably assisted by the famous vocalists
Mr. Plunket Greene and Miss Jean MacKinlay, who between them
rendered some dozen songs, old and new, in illustration of the van-
¥  ous examples cited by the lecturer, am idst great applause.

A‘ handsome illustrated souvenir programme, with cover designed
by George Morrow, was prepared for the occasion, containing a graphioc
% account of the literary work performed by members of the Society, from e -
. the pen of Miss Eleanor Hull, in which she says: ‘“ All that is best in Ty
% Trish literary, artistic and musical London may be said to be repre- ﬂ..
cented in the Society; we have amongst s pocts, musicians, critics, R,
and practical men., Their books, published within
the yeaps during which the Society has been in existence, would fill & 2
emall library. On our death-roll are the names of men and women P
who have helped to re-create what is best in the Ireland of to-day; on |
% our roll of the living are many who aro shaping the Ireland of the EIRET.
| In tho words of one of the original members, ‘‘ Kindred Irish

holidays, Limerick and its vicinity heing (he chosen locality. The.
party arrived on Iriday might, and were met by the Mayor and High,
Sheriff. On Saturday morning they started on a very interesting walk
round ** Old Limerick,” with' Dr. Fogerty actinlg as gulde, and 1n the
afternoon visited Killaloe, where they were shown over the Cathedral
by Canon Archdall. On Sunday afternoon tley motored to Adare and
Curragh Chase, where Mr. and Mrs. De Vere provided afternoon tea
and showed tham over the house and grounds. On Monday Quin Abbey
was visited under the guidance of Dr. McNamara, of Corofin,
the party drove to Dromoland Castle, where Lord and Lady Inchiguin
entertained them to luncheon and escorted them through the historic

whence

domain. Returning to Limerick the members dined with the Reception
Committeo, Archdeacon Hackett in the chair. Canon Langhridge pro-

posed the health of the visitors in happy terms, and Mr. A. P. Graves.
responded, giving some reminiscences of his boyhood’s days in the city,
S and referring to the many‘ literary celebrities who had heen born there.
_ Mr. T. W. Rolleston also rephed and later recited his fine poem ‘‘The
Abboy of Cong.”’

~ quo&tsion ln an article entitled *“ Peace or Givil War?” Mr. Darrell
h gais has a pleasant paper in the English ”’ on ‘¢ Irish Nationality,’*
ju which he tells the followi ing storny, which is wonth re-telling: ‘‘There -
f is an authentio story told of a certain living poet who, passing through
. 8 village in Galway with some friends, took them in to tea at a cot-
tage. He was hospitally eptertained, and when at the conclusion of
| the meal he asked what payment would be required of him, the response
ras & courteous refusal to charge him anything. ¢ Surely I know well
i who you are, Mr. X.,” said the hostess. ‘It’s a poet you are, and an
* homour to the country of Ireland, an it’s not for me to charge you any- |
thing at all.” It would be difficult to conceive of any other cou ntry
where such a thing would he possible.” ‘
¢ In “The Architectural Review > Mr. Harry Sirr has a most inter-
2 esting article on ¢ Eighteenthh Century Architecets and Artists in Ire-
© land,” full of hiographical information, the result of much pamst-akmg'

research. . , N

I notice that Miss I.etts, in her volume of verse reviewed elsewhere,

| pays a poetical tribute to Mr. Chas. I.. Graves, the literary adviser of - T
*:?:Més@rs Smith, Elder, and calls down ‘‘the blessings of bleasings for -5 m.
| him that has always time to be kind.” By a curious coincidence in .

: 1 “T P.’s Weekly,” Mr. F. T. Bullen, the nautical novelist, praises him : | :‘
* In plain: prose for the discrimination and insight that enabled him to e “‘
get his first book published after many rejectlon*s and thus started him i

; on the road to success. It is certainly a feather in the cap of any

3 ghterary « taster ”’ to have discovered and fostered two such able writers

ag Miss Lette and Mr. Bullen,

: The passing of the poet laureate, Alfred Awmstin, should not go un-

E soorded in these pages if for no other reason than to note the great
“la:nge that took place in his views regarding Ireland, as expressed in

B ;‘."= two works thercon. 1His Hibernian Horrors” (1880), is a vehem-

" ailrb attack upon Mr. Gladstone and all his Acts, Parnell and his deeds,
such as one would expect from & Conservative journalist as Austin then

206 ‘ THE ITRISH BOOK LOVER.

danins,”’ @ sarcastic attack on the powers that be in the church, law
and politics. The article on ‘‘The Oratory of Daniel O’Connell,”
founded on the chance meeting in the south of Ewmgland witlh an old
woman who remembered tho “Liberator,”’ tells us nothing new. “Mother
and Son,” by Daniel Corkery, is o fine and finished poem. *‘ The drish
Rosary ’’ contains an interesting account of Charlotte Grace O’Brien,
by W. P. Stockley, M.A., “one who knew her all his life,”” and some
valuable ** Notes on Medicine in Ireland,’” by that great physician and
Irish scholar, Dr. Norman Moore, whose portrait, by the way, hangs
“‘on the line”’ in this year’'s Royal Academy.

Another year has passed away—another 'solume ended—and once
more I take the opportunity of tendering my hearty thanks to the con-
tributors and subscribers who have so loyally and ungrudgingly co-
operated in what has been to them and me a labour of love. This little
journal has now made for itself a conspicuous and, it is to beo hoped,
a permanent place in periodical literature, with an ever increasing
circle of supporters, many: of whom have become personal friends.
I‘mm cormﬂpondence from all parts of the globe, I learn that i1ts ap-
pearance is cagerly looked for, month by month, and warmly w elcomed,
forming as it does, in the words of Stephen Gawynn, ‘“ A bond between
our hook-loving countrymen in the 'greater Ireland which has no
boundary.”” I.B.L. has not yet attained to my ideal, that there 18
great room for improvement mo one is more conscious or ready
acknowledge than the editor; but if the kindness of friends continues,
no efforts will be spared to render it still more worthy of their suppors
and commendation. |

REVIEWS.

Sones I'mom Leinstir. By W. M. Letts. (Smith, Elder.) Our ':-
zeaders know the admiration we have expressed from time to time of |

the humour, beauty and pathos that lie hidden in the scattered verses

of this gifted writer. They linger in our memory; and often come un-
biddon to our thoughts, Weo therefore hail this charming volume, tho
~ first fruits of her muse, which wall assure for ther a high place amongst
the younger singors of our day. Miss Letits rung the whole gamub |
of human emotion—the longing of the exilo for the * Irish Skies™s |
the widow’s sonrow for her “ Kind Companion ”’ ; the indecision of tho
_ ;ma.ldcanr's “* Ohoice”’ amongst her seven suitons; ennelﬂud ing with:—

aaslid: have Saint Joseph choose me a. comrade rich and kind—

‘Then there is the irresistible humour of ‘“ The Kerry (,ov., whose
proud mistress boasts :—

‘I theKing of Spain gets news of her he'll fill his purse with cold,
And set sail to ask the English King where she is to be sold. ‘
But the King of Spain may come to me, a crown upon his brow,
It is he may keep his golden purse—and I my Kerry cow.”

Or this sympathetic sorrow for the fisher lads at sea:—

“¢ There’s Patrick is in 1t, and Chnrsty, too,

A soft young lad, an’ he not sixteen—
‘An’ his brother drawmed last Hallow ¥’en.
God help his mother, what will she do?

* * * #* #

Thero is Daniel Connor and young Tom Byrne,

A~ Arps FARMER: or An Account of the Life of James Shanks. By
James C. Rutherford, B.A. (Belfast: Davidson & McCormack.) The
subject of this sketch, an carly and faithful supporter of 1.B.L., was
a striking example of self-culture and a many sided man. Though
mervely equipped with a national school education, by innate love of
| learning and constant application, he became a geologist and antiquary
= of more than local repute, surprising the ‘‘scientific gents’’ by the
e oxtent of his knowledge. A keen book lover, he had formed a library
of works on drreland, which was not equalled by any man of his class
—a cimple farmer—in his native country. In addition, he wae a born.
leader of men, one who nobly upheld the cause of the tenant farmer n

Issac Burr's Fazous Pameurner. (Vol. IV., p. 190.) In reply to
Mr. O’Donoghue’s inquiry regarding the once famous pamphlet, ‘“What

does She do with it? "’ there appears to have been no public ackmnow-

ledgment made, either on the part of Issac Butt himself or any of his
political associates that he was the writer of it. "Neither 1s there any
reference to it in his correspondence, of which several large collections
have been placed at my disposal. 1 was, however, informed last

October by Butt’s oldest surviving friend in London, the head of a

lange business in the City, that Butt wrote the pamphlet. This gen-

tleman also assured me that it was he who made the arrangements for

the printing of it, and that he had it issued through a firm of printers,
then in Mark Lane, named Boot & Co. It was published in 1871, and
was entitled ‘‘ Tracts for the Times, No. 1: /What does She do with
it? by Solomon Temple, Builder.”” It professed to make an expert ex-
amination of Queen Victoria’s private expenditure, and argued that
while her Ma:jes:i',y was constantly asking Parliament for money for her
<hildren, she was not spending the annuity originally secured to her by
the Civil List Act on the purposes for mibich it was designed. In those
days, following the formation of a Republic in France, a wave of
hostile criticism in England hegan to bhreak against the throne, and 2
republican agitation was praci}i-cai-bl y promoted amd led by Sir Charles

. Dilke, then Member of Parliament for Chelsea. At the same time,
owing to the Queen’s seclusion for very many years from social activity
and public duty, what it described as a. ‘‘ deep and universal feeling of =

discontent ”’ found voice in the English Pres 5. It is pointed out by

Sir Sidney Iee, in his Lifoe of Queen Victoria in the ‘¢ Dictionary of

National Biography,” vhat pamphlets, some of. which were attributed
to men of position, compared her income withi the modest allowance of

the President of the United States; and he singles out for special refer-

<nce that by ‘“ Solomon Temple,” describing iv as a malignant tract,

~ which enjoyed a great vogue. Ministers found it meedful to repudiate £
the damaging allegations made, and Sir Algernon West, a dmt-mgmslwd”

T’ easury official, was directed by Gladstone, who was then Primo M*m' A
rslber , Lo prepare an answer to the pamphlet. It should be noted tina&ﬁ

‘ﬁﬁfob to-ok no part in tho agitation carried on in England ; nor dld *fli"é"

OBITUARY.

| Epwarp GissoN, First Barox As:momim:, died suddenly in London
~on 22nd Magy, aged 76. Educated at T.C.D., he had a brilliant career
at the bar, and was three times Lord Chancellor of Ireland. In 1898 he
. published an interesting volume entitled ‘‘ Pitt: Some chapters of his
Laife and Times.”” This consists of a number of hitherto unpublished
. letters from the Bolton papers, which were placed at his disposal by
" Lord Bolton, the grandson of Thomas Orde, Chief Secretary (1785) of
i the famous ‘‘ Re solutions,” and the Orwell papers in the possession of
Mr. Pretyman, M.P>. He is succeeded by his cldest son, Hon. Wm.
- Gibson, President of the Gaelic League, and one time honorary secre-
tary to the Irishk Laterary Society. |
Joux Gonrnox Browx, horn at Carlow, 20th September, 1867, died
on 20th May. He acted as foreigw correspondent for ‘‘ The Times’’ in
4 various parts of the world. He nwas a brilliant writer, a man of fine ‘
*- £ <culture and literary tastes, cut off alk too soon.
: '4: Ricrarp ManrroLp Crare died in London on 5th May, aged 68S.

: bered the Army as surgeon and rotired with the rank of Lieut.-Colonel.

”Thoma@ ‘ The Sacrifice of Fools,” A Widow Well Left,” *‘All
Trum'ps,” “ Lanty Riordan’s Red Light,” *‘ A Torm Out Page,” etc.,

Miss Fmily Hickey will have ready in the antumn a new volume of
verse which Llkin Mathews ‘will publish. Miss Hickey, who took First
Class Honours at Cambridge, is a daughter of the late Canon Hickey,
of Macmines Castle, Enmiscorthy, and granddaughter of Rev. Wm. ==
Hickey, whose pen-name of ‘‘ Martin Doyle” was a household word in
the Ireland of the ecarly Victorian period. Miss Hickey has already &

published a dozen volumes or so.
